quotes.verbatim_quote:
The device is called a turnaround or curnback** and replaces the last rwo mea-
sures of motionless tonic. There are many kinds of turnarounds, involving different harmonic for-
mulas, although most will begin with a conic chord (but only for about two beats) and wil! end
with either a V7 chord (dominant) or a flat-I17 or flat-1M7 (dominant substitute.)*’
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage defines the turnaround device and specifies its structural rules — tonic start, two-beat limit, and dominant or dominant-substitute ending — giving players a formula to apply across contexts.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Because there is so often a tonic chord in the last two
measures, and because the beginning of new sections (or repeats of previous sections) often begins
with a tonic chord, a device is needed which would remove the excessive use of the tonic and at the
same time give the phrase-endings a sense of direction, namely to return gracefully to the beginning
of a repeated section.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This explains the functional necessity of the turnaround, grounding the technique in harmonic logic rather than mere convention.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
when the ninth is
added to the dominant seventh chord (1-3-5-} 7), it can also be augmented (+9) or minor (flat-9
—sometimes referred to as a diminished ninth):
quotes.why_load_bearing: This introduces the two altered-ninth options available on a dominant seventh chord, expanding the student's vocabulary beyond the basic major ninth.

quotes.topic: Dominant function retention with altered ninths
quotes.verbatim_quote:
In any case the chord retains its dominant function as long as the third is major and the seventh is
minor, regardless of the type of ninth used.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is a key rule stating the invariant condition for dominant function, allowing players to freely alter the ninth without disrupting harmonic direction.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Polychords® or bitonal triads*® (alternate term) exist when two chords are used simultaneously. In
the jazz idiom" the second chord, normally a triad, is added above the first chord, usually the given
chord and nearly always some kind of seventh chord, providing a very colorful effect; yet, the added
triad is chosen so as not to change the function of the bottom (given) chord.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This is the foundational definition of the chapter's central concept, establishing both what a polychord is and the governing constraint that the added triad must preserve the bottom chord's function.

quotes.topic: Two methods for choosing added triad
quotes.verbatim_quote:
proper choice of
the added triad is achieved in one of two ways: either by using both the given chord and one of its
substitutions; or by locating a second chord whose tones are made up of ninths, elevenths, or thir-
teenths of the given chord plus, perhaps, a tone or two from the given chord itself,
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage gives players two concrete, actionable methods for constructing polychords, making the concept practically applicable rather than merely theoretical.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
It has already
been stated that ninths may be major, minor, or augmented when applied to the dominant seventh.
It is also true that the fifth of a dominant seventh may be unaltered (perfect), lowered (diminished),
or raised (augmented), without changing the chord’s function. The eleventh can be perfect or aug-
mented, and the thirteenth is usually major, though in rare cases it can be lowered (minor thirteenth).
quotes.why_load_bearing: This catalogs the full set of available tensions and alterations on a dominant seventh, which is the raw material from which polychord upper structures are drawn.

quotes.verbatim_quote:
Beginning with Pattern No. 180, a large number of polychordal possibilities are investigated as
well as methods for putting polychords together in patterns that will permit a non-keyboard instru-
ment to sound two chords together by alternation.
quotes.why_load_bearing: This passage identifies the specific technical challenge for non-keyboard players and signals that the following patterns solve it through alternation, orienting practice toward a guitaristic solution.
